items.summaryZh: 文章探讨了自动化对经济活动的影响，指出若没有真实世界的任务数据，AI模型只能在实验室内部提升表现，难以实现真实商业价值，提醒投资者关注哪些行业的工作仍不可被自动化取代。
items.translationZh: 昨天的文章以一个我尚未解决的问题收尾：人类可以同时保持在人机交互的环节中，而保持在环节中的经济效益却在恶化。Shipper 的“框架-框架制定者”论点给了我一个哲学上的理由去怀疑自动化的灾难论，但如果你的薪水系于模型即将攀升的框架，哲学的慰藉是凉薄的。你可以去 Mercor 查看哪些技能正在被强化学习（RL）淘汰。来源：Mercor。自动化如何影响不同行业和公司将是未来需要重点思考的问题。正如我昨天所说，数万亿美元正被每年用于自动化相当一部分当前的知识工作。Tom Reed 上个月写了一篇很好的文章，提出我们可能在追求他所谓的“Goodhart 奇点”。Reed 对自动化灾难的反驳异常简单：没有接触到问题来源，就无法做好解决问题的能力，而大多数问题的唯一来源是与现实世界的缓慢、昂贵的交互。没有这种接触，递归循环只能产生远不如宣传的成果。Reed 文章中的一句话：“一个孤立的数据中心的天才所产生的研发输出，只会是一个 Goodhart 奇点。一个在基准上自我改进的孤立 AI 看似在逼近超智能，却实际上只是在优化评价表现，无法在实验室之外泛化。”为什么自我改进会在实验室之外停滞？因为模型擅长练习它们所练习的内容，而对大多数经济价值工作而言，练习的对象根本不存在。Reed 最具启发性的观察是关于数据本身的存在：“对经济中的大多数任务来说，预训练语料库里有关于任务的论述，却没有任务本身的记录。这也是代码进展快于其他领域的原因之一——代码几乎完全可以归结为其 token 序列。”互联网充斥着评论和建议，却从未把完成并购交易或决定哪款无人机原型投产的实际步骤序列化为 token。自然的反驳是足够聪明的系统可以模拟它缺失的数据。Reed 对此持怀疑态度：“考虑到几乎一半的 SWE‑Bench 提交被 AI 自动评分器接受，却会被相应仓库的真实维护者拒绝。能够在不提升实际合并率的情况下提升 SWE‑Bench 分数，对我而言暗示了数据中心天才将面临的局面。”伟大的郑东（great Zhengdong）对 AI 研究本身的进展作了同样的说明。模型唯一能变好的只有‘评估’，而‘研究者本人’只想优化……他们只想要一个重要的问题、一个清晰的评估来衡量进展，然后去优化它。我认为 AI 公司需要真实世界的部署作为问题来源，否则它们将没有好的优化目标。信号由数以百万计的市场参与者通过行为揭示其偏好，只有在部署产生之前根本不存在。如果 Reed 说得对，扩散本身就是能力的输入。基准框架之所以可攀，是因为它是冻结的，但经济是否会停止产生新的‘框架’？标准的反对意见是‘样本效率’：数据今天不存在，但如果模型变得更高效学习者，自动化的研究者是否会破解学习本身的问题。Dwarkesh 最近写了一篇文章，提到样本效率并不是模型改进的关键来源：“智能的一个定义是样本效率——即在给定领域需要观察多少数据才能流畅且胜任地工作。过去几年我们并没有在训练样本效率上取得多大进展——更像是我们显著扩大并改进了数据分布。AI 变好的主要方式是添加更多更好的数据，并扩展算力来生成这些数据。显而易见，强化学习（RL）是主要方式。你可以把 RL 看作一种合成数据生成——你对一个验证器倾倒大量算力，以寻找‘好’数据。然后你训练模型去预测这些正确的 rollout，方式与预测互联网上的下一个词相似。”一旦把 RL 看作针对验证器的合成数据生成，你就能理解模型在每个领域都需要大量的人类专家示范。Dwarkesh 同时也认为样本低效对大块白领工作可能并不重要。模型学习在数十亿次会话中摊销，因此即使训练非常低效，仍能在实验室收入曲线展示的巨额经济意义上可行。把 Reed 与 Dwarkesh 并排放在一起，会出现一种循环：数据中心的研究者需要真实多样的问题集来评估候选学习算法是否更好，而这个问题集恰恰是数据中心在许多领域所缺乏的。因此，如果能力通过部署实现，而制造的真实世界信号的替代品既昂贵又定制化，那么价值会流向何处？Sarah Guo 在一篇有趣的文章中探讨了这个问题，她认为任何可以衡量且可以训练的东西，都已在走向商品化。她的 2×2 矩阵围绕两个轴：答案是公开可验证的还是存于私有数据，任务是仍在前沿还是已趋于饱和。Guo 文章中的一句话：“我们可以对任何工作提出两个问题。其正确性是私有且昂贵建立的，仅存在于某人数据中吗？它是否被封闭在你无法进入的系统内部？将这些与任务是否已饱和对应，你得到一个 2×2。饱和且公开答案的工作是商品化的 token，开放模型拥有它。前沿且公开答案的工作——代码基准所在——是实验室获胜的领域，因为当评估免费时，拥有它毫无意义。奖品是最后一个角落：不可训练的前沿工作，其正确性仅存在于私有中。你可以在托管 AI 本土先驱的推理云中看到，大多数 token 是由定制模型而非通用开放模型生成的……能力消耗许多东西，但更好的模型并不能让私有的真实答案公开。它没有许可证、签署责任，也不拥有公司的文件，并且在答案错误时它不能成为被起诉的一方。这里的瓶颈不是智能，而是许可和问责。你可以想象一个比任何人都聪明的模型，它仍然需要被允许进入门内，并且仍然需要有人为它的行为签名。”代码首先成熟是因为编译器和测试套件是免费验证器，但即便如此，Guo 引用了 MIT 研究跨越十万开发者的工作：编码代理提升了约 180% 的代码编写量，但实际交付的代码仅增长约 30%。这两个数字之间的差距是工作中仍难以自动化的不可读部分。对非 AI 实验室而言，最大的奖品是正确性仅存在于公司私有数据中的前沿工作。这个 2×2 框架是思考实验室可能对任何软件公司构成风险的有用方式。为了更形象，Latent Space 将此 2×2 框架做成了信息图（见下）。来源：Latent Space。订阅者可每日获取日志以及五年以上的深度分析，即对 65+ 家公司的完整财务模型。每日内容只是我在深度分析之间的思考过程！订阅 当前投资组合：请注意，这些并非我的买入/卖出建议，仅是披露，以便你评估因我个人持仓可能产生的偏见。请始终把我的写作视为个人投资日志，切勿忘记我的目标、风险承受能力和约束可能与你不同。我的当前投资组合已在下方披露。

items.author: https://www.mbi-deepdives.com/feed
items.title: Automation's Asymptote
items.textEn: Anthropic launched “Claude Fable 5” yesterday which is the much anticipated “Mythos-class” model. Anthropic was already leading in most benchmark with its Opus 4.8 model, but Fable 5 created a bit more distance from its competitors. Anthropic’s pace of model release even when its setting the bar at the frontier does give some credence to the notion of “recursive self improvement” of building models. Staring at the benchmark chart may make you wonder whether we are indeed at the cusp of automating a good chunk of white collar work. Source: Anthropic Even OpenAI recently explicitly laid out an audacious vision : “ Our internal belief is that by March of 2028 we may have a significant fraction of our research being done by AI systems in tandem with our own researchers .” When trillion+ dollars are expected to be deployed on an annual basis to build and serve these models, you perhaps do need these eyebrows raising goals in not-so-distant future. Nonetheless, as the model capabilities are increasing over time, it is perhaps reasonable to feel increasing discomfort due to fear of automation. During my break, I read this very thoughtful piece: “ After Automation ” by Every’s Dan Shipper who made the case that you can be simultaneously AI-pilled and yet not fear the impending automation doom. He also appeared on Lenny’s podcast to discuss the piece. I have read and listened to both and frankly speaking, I found it to be one of the best articulations tackling this topic. So, I recommend you take the time and either read the full piece or listen to the podcast. I do want to highlight a few bits from Shipper’s piece that I found to be quite compelling. One of the highlights from his piece is a discussion on in-house benchmark that Shipper came up with and how the score on that benchmark evolved: “We built an in-house benchmark called the Senior Engineer benchmark. It is, as its name implies, designed to test how good frontier models are at senior engineer–level coding tasks like a major refactor. The Senior Engineer benchmark gives a coding agent a vibe coded production codebase that has gone sideways. It’s from a real codebase for Proof that I vibe coded and subsequently needed a senior engineer to fix. The agent gets the codebase as it was before it was fixed and is the kind of instructions you’d give a senior engineer: “This is vibe coded slop; please rewrite it from first principles.” This is a good benchmark because it tests the ability for a coding agent to examine many different, unrelated problems and then sees whether it has enough autonomy, conceptual clarity, and courage to perform a working rewrite. (I also have two rewrites from human senior engineers, who used AI, that I use to compare and grade the model output.) Coding agents find this task hard. Not only does the agent need to find the root of the problem, it needs to keep the problem in mind over many turns without getting distracted by existing code. It also needs to be comfortable deleting large portions of the codebase—which agents are trained to avoid. Most coding agents can identify the shape of the rewrite, but when it comes to execution, they patch over the problem instead of fixing it. Until GPT-5.5 . GPT-5.5 scored a 62/100 on its best run—about 30 points above Opus 4.7. (6) GPT-5.5’s result felt like the model has crossed a line: not autocomplete, not assistant, not tool, but something uncomfortably close to a human. A human senior engineer scores in the high 80s or low 90s on the benchmark, so another 30 points and it’ll be at human senior engineer level. That is how benchmark numbers work on the imagination: They turn a strange, qualitative change into a clean number that tells a powerful—scary—story. (Next stop: chart psychosis.) My guess is that the models will hit the 80s and 90s on this benchmark within the next year. But it is important to understand what the score contains in order to tell us what it means. In this case, the 62 isn’t just a measure of the model itself.” It turns out even Shipper underestimated the pace of improvement as he revealed yesterday that Fable 5 scored 91 on this benchmark! To his credit, Shipper never intended to be married to a particular timeline of when the benchmark will be saturated by the models. He, in fact, laid out why the score itself reveals far less than what most people may think. Again, from his piece: The prompt for the Senior Engineer benchmark is generic, but it is a frame. And if we varied it, we would see the model perform at a different level. For example, the prompt asks for a “structural rewrite from first principles,” it says the problem is likely in the “document collaboration” part of the code, and it asks the coding agent to find and hold to “invariants.” If we removed those particulars, the score would go down. If we replaced the prompt entirely with one asking the model to “solve all of the errors that keep popping up,” the model’s score would be close to zero. It would go straight to identifying and resolving the issues one by one, instead of taking a step back to consider a rewrite. (8) I can also trivially raise the model’s score. If I ask it to delete a lot of code and give it exact filenames that should be pared down, or if I ask it to check the results of its work to make sure the app is fully functional before it says it’s done, it will be better at the task. …Once the current Senior Engineer benchmark saturates, we’ll change the frame to zero it out again. The next benchmark will not ask only, “Can you rewrite the app?” It will ask: Can you decide when a rewrite is needed, choose the scope, preserve the right invariants, manage the migration, and judge whether the result is any good? As senior engineers use AI to solve these problems, the models will get better at solving them on their own. We will all momentarily freak out. It looks like the model can now decide whether to do a rewrite! They can do everything a senior engineer can do! And then a new edge will appear that was not obvious before, we will zero our benchmarks, demand will stimulate, and the process will repeat Shipper framed the dynamic as Zeno’s paradox : humanity is the tortoise with a fifty-yard head start, the model is Achilles, and every time the model reaches where we stood, we have moved because saturating a benchmark immediately prompts us to redraw it. Progress inside any fixed “frame” is exponential, but progress against the moving frontier of what humans actually value behaves like an asymptote. Shipper makes more of a philosophical point here, but it does ring true to me: “The panic that AI generates when we observe it doing something new keeps coming back to this: We set a frame, watch the models climb it, and then confuse the frame—or whatever climbs it—with the thing itself. When we look at a benchmark and compare it to human abilities, we confuse the frame for the framer. The score tells us how well the model operates inside a frame we supplied; it does not tell us that the model has become us. That is the category error underneath the panic. We point to the latest edge we drew and say: This is us. Then, when the model climbs it, it feels like it has caught us. But it has caught the frame, not the framer. The mistake is wanting something concrete to hold on to. We want to say: Intelligence is this benchmark, but the moment something is concrete enough to point at, it is concrete enough to climb. Frames are necessary; they let us get traction on the world. But they are frozen, partial, and therefore optimizable. Framers are different. The framer is the one still in contact with what the frame has to discard—the whole situation as it appears to them, moment to moment. What is this “whole situation”? The moment you start to say what “the whole situation” contains, you have already begun another frame. You can’t say what “it” is, but it exists because you exist.” I do wonder, however, that it can be simultaneously true that humans remain in this loop and that the economics of being in the loop can deteriorate. If reviewing AI-generated pull requests becomes the core of the engineering job, the supply of people who can do that job adequately may expand faster than the demand for it, especially when the models themselves are being trained, release by release, on exactly the review-and-framing behavior we perform. To what extent most people can retain or improve their economic worth in a world where AI models continue to climb the hill is perhaps an open question. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! Subscribe Disclaimer: All posts on “MBI Deep Dives” are for informational purposes only. This is NOT a recommendation to buy or sell securities discussed. Please do your own work before investing your money.
items.summaryZh: 文章讨论了Anthropic新模型Claude Fable 5的突破以及AI在高阶工程任务上快速逼近人类水平，强调了基准测试框架的局限性和自动化对白领工作潜在冲击，对投资者判断AI技术商业化进程及其对相关行业的影响具有参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：Automation's Asymptote（自动化的渐近线）

正文：Anthropic 昨天推出了“Claude Fable 5”，这是一款备受期待的“Mythos‑class”模型。Anthropic 之前凭借其 Opus 4.8 模型已在大多数基准测试中领先，但 Fable 5 与竞争对手的差距更大。即使在设定前沿标准的情况下，Anthropic 的模型发布节奏仍让人对模型的“递归自我改进”（recursive self improvement）概念产生信服。盯着基准图表不禁让人思考，我们是否真的站在自动化相当一部分白领工作 的拐点上。

来源：Anthropic

即便是 OpenAI 最近也明确阐述了一个大胆的愿景：“我们内部的信念是，到 2028 年 3 月，AI 系统可能已经与我们的研究人员一起完成我们相当大比例的研究工作”。

当预计每年将有上万亿美元用于构建和提供这些模型时，这类令人眉毛挑起的目标在不久的将来似乎并非不可能。尽管模型能力随时间提升，但因自动化带来的不安感也在加剧。

在我的休息时间，我读到了一篇非常深思的文章：《After Automation》——作者是 Every’s Dan Shipper，他在文中提出，即使你对 AI 痴迷，也不必恐惧即将到来的自动化厄运。他随后还在 Lenny 的播客中讨论了该文。我的确阅读并收听了两者，坦率地说，我觉得这是迄今为止对这一主题最好的阐释之一。因此，我建议你抽时间阅读完整文章或收听播客。

我想强调 Shipper 文章中的几段我觉得尤为发人深省。文中一个亮点是他自创的内部基准——Senior Engineer Benchmark（高级工程师基准）。正如其名，这个基准旨在测试前沿模型在高级工程师级别编码任务（如大型重构）上的表现。该基准让一个编码代理面对一段已经出现问题的真实代码库（来源于 Proof 项目的代码），并给出类似高级工程师的指令：“这段代码很乱，请从第一原则重新编写”。这是一项优秀的基准，因为它检验编码代理在面对众多不相关问题时，是否具备足够的自主性、概念清晰度以及大胆进行完整改写的能力。（我还准备了两份人类高级工程师使用 AI 完成的重写，以供比较和评分。）

编码代理觉得这项任务很难。代理不仅需要找出问题根源，还要在多轮交互中保持对问题的关注，而不是被现有代码分心；它还必须愿意删除大量代码——而模型的训练倾向是避免这样做。大多数编码代理只能识别出需要重写的形态，但在实际执行时往往是补丁式解决而非根本修复。直到 GPT‑5.5 出现。GPT‑5.5 在最佳运行中得分 62/100，约比 Opus 4.7 高出 30 分。（6）GPT‑5.5 的结果让人感觉模型已经跨过了一条线：既不是自动补全，也不是助理，也不是工具，而是令人不安地接近人类。人类高级工程师在该基准上得分在 80‑90 分左右，再提升约 30 分就能达到人类高级工程师水平。这就是基准分数的想象力：它把一种奇异、定性的变化转化为一个干净的数字，讲述出一个强大且令人恐惧的故事。（下一站：图表妄想症。）

我的猜测是模型将在未来一年内在该基准上达到 80‑90 分。但理解这些分数所包含的内容很重要。正如文中所言，62 分不仅仅是模型本身的衡量。

事实证明，Shipper 甚至低估了改进的速度——他昨天透露 Fable 5 在该基准上得了 91 分！值得称赞的是，Shipper 并未打算把模型何时饱和于此基准绑定到特定时间线。他实际上阐明了为什么该分数本身揭示的信息远低于大多数人可能的想象。再次引用他的文章：Senior Engineer Benchmark 的提示语是通用的，但它本身就是一个框架。如果我们更改提示语，模型的表现会不同。例如，这个提示要求“从第一原则进行结构性重写”，指出问题可能出在代码的“文档协作”部分，并要求编码代理保持“不变式”。如果去掉这些细节，得分会下降；如果把提示改成“解决所有不断出现的错误”，模型的得分几乎为零，因为它会逐一识别并解决问题，而不是退后一步考虑整体重写。（8）我甚至可以轻易提升模型的得分——如果我让它删除大量代码并给出确切的文件名，或要求它在宣布完成前检查结果是否完全可用，它的表现会更好……一旦当前的 Senior Engineer Benchmark 达到饱和，我们将重新设定框架，使其再次归零。下一个基准不会仅仅问“你能重写这个应用吗？”，而会问：“你能决定何时需要重写、选择范围、保持正确的不变式、管理迁移并判断结果是否良好吗？”随着高级工程师使用 AI 解决这些问题，模型也会在自行解决这些任务上变得更好。我们都会短暂惊慌：看起来模型现在可以自行决定是否进行重写！它们可以完成高级工程师能做的一切！随后会出现一个之前未曾察觉的新边界，我们会把基准归零，需求刺激模型进步，过程再次循环。

Shipper 将这种动态描述为 Zeno（芝诺）的悖论：人类是拥有 50 码领先优势的乌龟，模型是阿基里斯，每当模型赶上我们所在的位置时，我们已经向前移动，因为基准一旦被饱和就会立刻被重新绘制。任何固定“框架”内部的进展是指数级的，但相对于人类真实价值的移动前沿的进展表现为渐近线。

Shipper 在哲学层面提出了更深的观点，这点让我深有共鸣：“AI 在我们观察到它做出新事物时产生的恐慌，总是回到这样一个点：我们设定一个框架，观察模型在框架内攀爬，然后把框架本身或攀爬框架的东西误认为是模型本身。当我们看到基准并把它与人类能力比较时，我们把框架误认为是框架的设计者。分数告诉我们模型在我们提供的框架内运作得多好；它并不说明模型已经变成了我们。那是恐慌背后的类别错误。我们指向最新的我们画出的边界，然后说：这就是我们。当模型攀上它时，感觉像是它追上了我们。但它追上的是框架，而不是框架的设计者。错误在于我们想要一个具体的、可抓住的东西。我们想说：智能就是这个基准，但当某件事物足够具体以至于可以指向时，它也足够具体以至于可以被攀登。框架是必要的，它们让我们能够在世界上获得抓地力。但它们是冻结的、局部的，因此可以被优化。框架的制定者才是不同的。制定者仍与框架必须抛弃的内容保持接触——即他们眼前瞬间出现的整个情境。什么是‘整个情境’？当你开始描述‘整个情境’包含什么时，你已经开始了另一套框架。你无法说‘它’是什么，但它存在因为你存在。”

我确实在思考，虽然人类仍在这个循环中，但在循环中的经济价值可能会恶化。如果审查 AI 生成的 pull request 成为工程师工作的核心，那么能够胜任这项工作的人的供应可能会比需求增长更快，尤其是当模型本身在每一次发布中都在训练、学习我们所执行的审查与框架行为时。大多数人在一个 AI 模型持续攀登的世界里，能够保持或提升自身经济价值的程度，或许仍是个未解之谜。

订阅者可以每日收到简报以及五年多的深度报告（即对 65+ 家公司的全长分析与财务模型）。每日简报只是我在深度报告之间的思考记录！

订阅须知：所有 “MBI Deep Dives” 的文章均仅供参考。本文并非对涉及的证券买卖的推荐。请在投资前自行做研究。

items.title: Paul Kennedy on Great Powers
items.textEn: What a profound honor to have Paul Kennedy on the ChinaTalk podcast. Kennedy is my favorite living historian and the writer who’s most shaped my intellectual development. His analysis underpins what you hear on this show every week. The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers is an epochal work that traces global power transitions from 1500 to the present. It’s gripping, forest-and-trees scholarship at its finest. Equally impressive in different ways is his book, The Rise of Anglo-German Antagonism, 1860 to 1914 . Not only is it god-tier diplomatic history, but it also gives you a feel for the era through its explorations of social, economic, domestic, political, and cultural dimensions of Anglo-German relations. There are fascinating US/China analogies that we’ll get into in this podcast. His two most recent works directly inform the military coverage on ChinaTalk. Engineers of Victory looks at how people and the systems they worked within solved engineering challenges that turned the tide for entire theaters in World War II. His latest, Victory at Sea: Naval Power and the Transformation of Global Order in World War II , is a sweeping history of a radical transformation in the balance of military power, from the mid-1930s when America was just gaining prominence, to after World War II, when it had no other significant naval competitor. The Parliament of Man: A History of the United Nations made me interested in international organizations and gave me my senior thesis topic about the creation of the UN. What Kennedy taught me more than anything is this — sweat the details, look at the individual players, and zoom out often enough to understand what truly shapes the long-term fate of nations. Over the course of this episode, we pick up themes from all across his work: Great Power rivalries of the late 19th-early 20th centuries, the China echoes today, and why potential antagonisms turn nice and others turn belligerent, The persistent struggles of liberal internationalists and why they rarely get the outcomes they want, Why China today is not Germany of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, The surprising ways geography shapes global power dynamics, How fear spreads among nations and why mutual suspicion is so hard to escape, Why top powers blow it and lose their dominant place in the world, How systems and innovation win wars. And much more, including salutary lessons from the Dutch and Swedes on boring yet prosperous futures, how Churchill’s interest in gadgets influenced the course of the Second World War, and why transformative action from the UN remains unlikely in the near future. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . How to Win Friends and Alienate Nations Jordan Schneider: What is national power, and why does it matter? Paul Kennedy: Since the earliest struggles between tribes and later between larger groups of people with their symbols and more organized armies, and in particular, the emergence of the modern nation-state around the year 1500 (as historians roughly date it) — this entity has existed. The nation-state, with its capacity to raise taxes, conscript, and enroll men to fight under the direction of, say, a Dutch republic or a Spanish monarchy, has fundamentally shaped global affairs. Since that era, with the rise of the nation-state in Western Europe and its subsequent adoption worldwide, this thing we call "the country," "the nation," or "the state," has operated within what political scientists term an anarchic international system. "Anarchy" here doesn’t necessarily mean everyone slaughtering everyone else, but simply the absence of a higher authority controlling global affairs. The United Nations General Assembly lists approximately 189 or 192 nation-states, large and small. We give them all a membership, a vote in the General Assembly. Almost all of them have some sort of diplomatic service as well as a domestic home service, have a government and a constitution of one form or another, and interact with the other nation-states, the other players in the system. Because the decisions they take over time impact individuals in different ways, we pay attention to this, to international history. We pay attention because this great power system, in terms of a relatively harmonious relationship between the nation-states, broke down into massive, expensive, and bloody warfare in the First and Second World Wars. It behooves us to understand this great power system and the nation-states which play such a role within the system. Jordan Schneider: I want to begin with a quote from a German politician in 1903, which comes back to your idea of tribes. Eduard Bernstein , in a debate between the left and right in the Reichstag over Anglo-German trade relations said, "The entire question is this: When do we consider the position of one land to another in the manner of jealous, acquisitive tribes, which if one robs, the other is robbed, and when do we consider it from the standpoint of a peaceful exchange of nations?" Paul Kennedy: A nice quote from Bernstein, who was one of the leaders of the left liberal and progressive parties in Imperial Germany, which were somewhat swept away with the coming of war in 1914. But he was still optimistic then. You can tell from his question in that Reichstag debate that he was presenting essentially two large, contradictory, and distinct versions of understanding international affairs. Was international affairs to be regarded as a benign relationship between these advanced states and societies in which you traded with each other? Great Britain in 1903, when Bernstein was speaking, was the number one commercial partner of Imperial Germany. Germany relied heavily upon exporting many of its manufactured goods to the UK market. The UK exported a large amount of imperial and tropical produce to the German market. This harmonious interchange of commercial goods between states, as well as the harmonious interchange of their ideas, university students, travelers, tourists — that’s the benign view of international relations. The more malign, suspicious, or competitive view is that these are rival tribes. They are in some sort of inherent, natural fighting, suspicious relationship with each other. So one is a hostile relationship and the other is benign and friendly. As the years evolved towards 1914, these two interpretations of international affairs were strongly contested because if one won and prevailed, all would be well, benign trade would rise. If the other was true, then you had to keep your weapons sharp. You had to keep your navies big and strong. You had to have intelligence systems gathering information about what the other might be up to. You had to be suspicious rather than trusting. Those are two very different views of world affairs. Jordan Schneider: The answer, of course, is both. Humanity would not have been able to reach the heights it is today if we hadn’t figured out how to cooperate with each other, but looking over the past 5,000 years, there are moments in time when that mindset shifts, particularly when it comes to great powers. You raise this fascinating counterfactual of England not being anxious about America’s rise, even though they have a past history of fighting wars. The "ties that bind" — but there were plenty of those also between Germany and the United Kingdom. Is there a theory about when the mindsets start to shift from "we can cooperate with these guys" to "we can’t really in the end"? Paul Kennedy: This is a question I pose to my students interested in international affairs and why systems break down. In the late 19th century, the number one industrial trading naval power of Great Britain was finding the world less and less comfortable because its relative share was diminishing, and the two quickly rising countries which were diminishing those shares were Imperial Germany on one side and the United States of America on the other side. Yet, as you allude to, the story is that the British turn to an ever-more-friendly, almost contractual relationship with the United States and to an ever-less-friendly, ever-more-suspicious relationship with Imperial Germany. Why should Great Britain, with these relatively sane and intelligent decision-makers in Whitehall, turn against one and turn in favor to the other? You can consider some of the major and obvious reasons and ask students to try to weigh them or evaluate their significance. One is geography — sheer distance. Even if it is a rising great power, if it is 3,000 miles away, that’s less of a problem than if this rising great power is just 15 hours steaming across the North Sea in the middle of the night. If that rising power on the other side of the Atlantic, as it threatened to be back in the 1840s and 1860s, might gobble up your dominion state of Canada, that would be different. But by 1903 or so, American-Canadian relations and disputes over borders and seal fisheries and other things had been resolved. So there was no hostile threat from this distant rising power across the Atlantic. Historians also point out the enormous advantage of a common language . You could speak to each other and feel part of a longstanding English cultural world, despite the fact the United States was a polyglot nation with all sorts of cultural inputs. Around the turn of the century, identification with Anglo culture was predominant among American elites. That helped. A good number of American politicians, from Teddy Roosevelt onward, grew up very much in this Anglo-historical tradition, and on the British side, once the elderly, suspicious Lord Salisbury had passed, there were also quite a few committed Americanophiles. Then there is the issue of ideology — an autocratic nation on one side (Imperial Germany) and a democratic parliamentary or congressional nation on the other. So you can start listing a whole number of reasons why it was relatively easy for the British to see the r…
items.summaryZh: 文章介绍了历史学家保罗·肯尼迪在ChinaTalk播客的访谈，重点回顾了他关于大国兴衰、英德冲突及美中关系的研究，强调了解大国竞争与合作的历史逻辑对投资者判断地缘政治风险的重要性。
items.translationZh:
标题：保罗·肯尼迪谈大国

正文：能够请到保罗·肯尼迪参加ChinaTalk播客，我感到无比荣幸。肯尼迪是我最喜欢的在世历史学家，也是对我的知识体系影响最大的作者。他的分析是本节目每周内容的基石。《大国的兴衰》（The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers）是划时代的著作，追踪了从1500年至今的全球权力转移，是扣人心弦、宏观与微观兼备的学术佳作。同样令人印象深刻的是他的另一部作品《英德对立的崛起，1860‑1914》（The Rise of Anglo‑German Antagonism），这是一部顶级的外交史著作，同时通过对英德关系的社会、经济、国内政治和文化维度的探讨，让人感受到那个时代的氛围。书中有许多关于美中类比的有趣点，我们将在本期播客中展开讨论。

他的两部最新作品直接为ChinaTalk的军事报道提供了视角：《胜利工程师》（Engineers of Victory）研究了二战期间人们及其所在系统如何破解工程难题，从而扭转整个战场的局势；最新的《海上胜利：海军力量与二战后全球秩序的转变》（Victory at Sea: Naval Power and the Transformation of Global Order in World War II）则是对从1930年代中期美国刚刚崭露头角，到二战结束后美国已无显著海军竞争者这一过程的宏大叙事。《人类议会》（The Parliament of Man: A History of the United Nations）让我对国际组织产生兴趣，并成为我关于联合国创建的毕业论文题目。

肯尼迪教给我最重要的一点是——关注细节，审视个体玩家，同时又要经常抽离出来，理解真正决定国家长期命运的因素。在本期节目中，我们将梳理他作品中的多个主题：19世纪末‑20世纪初的大国竞争、当今中国的回响、为何某些潜在冲突会转为友好而另一些则演变为敌对、自由主义国际主义者的持续困境以及他们为何常常难以实现预期目标、为何当代中国并非19、20世纪末的德国、地理如何出人意料地塑造全球权力格局、恐惧如何在国家间传播以及相互猜疑为何难以摆脱、为何霸主会失误并失去统治地位、系统与创新如何决定战争胜负等，此外还包括荷兰和瑞典在乏味却繁荣的未来中的启示、丘吉尔对小工具的兴趣如何影响二战进程、以及为什么联合国在近期实现转型行动的可能性仍然渺茫。立即在您喜爱的播客应用中收听。

如何赢得朋友并疏远国家
Jordan Schneider：什么是国家力量，为什么它重要？
Paul Kennedy：自从部落之间最早的冲突以及后来的更大群体之间的冲突——有了象征和更组织化的军队，尤其是大约在1500年左右出现的现代民族国家（historian大致的划分）——这种实体就一直存在。民族国家具备征税、征兵的能力，能够在如荷兰共和国或西班牙王室的指挥下动员人们参战，这根本塑造了全球事务。自那时起，随着民族国家在西欧的崛起并随后被全世界采用，我们所谓的“国家”“民族”或“政体”，在政治学家称之为“无政府国际体系”的框架中运作。这里的“无政府”并不一定意味着人人相残，而是指缺乏一个最高权威来统辖全球事务。联合国大会列出了大约189至192个各式国家，大小不等。我们给予它们成员资格、在大会中的投票权。几乎所有国家都有某种外交机构以及国内机构，拥有政府和宪法（或类似形式），并与其他国家——即体系中的其他玩家——互动。因为它们的决策随时间对个人产生不同影响，我们关注这些国际历史。我们之所以关注，是因为这种大国体系——在相对和谐的国家间关系层面——在第一次和第二次世界大战中崩溃，导致大规模、昂贵且血腥的战争。了解这一大国体系及其内部的国家角色，对我们而言是有益的。
Jordan Schneider：我想引用一位1903年德国政治家的话，这与您对部落的概念呼应。爱德华·本恩斯坦（Eduard Bernstein）在议会（Reichstag）关于英德贸易关系的左右翼辩论中说道：“整个问题在于：我们何时像妒忌、抢夺的部落那样看待一块土地与另一块土地的关系？若一方被抢劫，另一方也被抢劫；何时我们从和平的民族交换立场来看待它？”
Paul Kennedy：这是本恩斯坦的好句子，他是德意志帝国时期左翼自由进步党的领袖之一，但在1914年战争爆发后这些党派基本消失。不过那时他仍保持乐观。从他的议会质询可以看出，他提出了两种截然不同、相互矛盾的国际事务理解方式。第一种是把国际事务视为这些先进国家与社会之间的良性关系，彼此进行贸易；第二种则是把它们视为竞争部落，天然地互相警惕、敌对。1903年，英国是德国的头号商业伙伴，德国大量制造品出口至英国市场，英国则向德国出口大量帝国和热带产品。这种商品与思想、学生、旅行者的和谐交换代表了友好型国际关系。而另一种则是把对方视为潜在敌手，需要保持海军强大、情报系统发达，并保持警惕。这两种截然不同的视角决定了各国的政策取向。
Jordan Schneider：答案显然是两者兼而有之。如果没有合作，人类不可能达到今天的高度；但回顾过去5000年，往往在大国兴起时，这种合作心态会发生转变。您提到的英国对美国崛起并不焦虑的反事实也很有趣。当时英国与德国之间也存在大量联系。是否有理论解释这种从“我们可以合作”到“我们最终无法合作”的心态转变？
Paul Kennedy：这是我在课堂上经常提出给国际事务学生的问题——系统何时崩溃。19世纪后期，英国作为工业、贸易和海军强国，因相对份额下降而感到不安；而崛起的德国和美国正蚕食其份额。英国对美国逐渐采取更友好、准合同式的关系，而对德国则日益警惕。为何在白厅（Whitehall）的决策者相对理智的情况下，英国会对一方友好、对另一方敌视？原因包括地理距离——跨大西洋的美国与英国相距3000英里，远不如在北海上仅数小时航程的德国那样近；语言共同体——英美共享语言与文化传统，政治精英多认同盎格鲁文化；意识形态差异——德国是君主专制体制，而美国是民主议会制。上述种种因素综合，使得英国在当时更易于与美国形成亲密关系，而对德国保持警惕。

items.title: FICO: The Contestable Monopoly?
items.textEn: This is part-3 of my FICO series. In Part 1 , I traced the company’s history through the 2018-2025 pricing-power era and pushed back on the popular narrative that FICO’s monopoly was simply gifted to it by the 1995 GSE decision. In Part 2 , I went deep on the actual mechanics of how FICO gets paid in mortgage, walked through the new pricing, and explained how the score business has eyewatering margins. Today, I want to tackle behind the paywall what I think is the most important question for the stock: how contestable is this monopoly, actually? Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! 标题：FICO：可争议的垄断？

正文：这是我关于FICO系列的第三篇。在第一部分，我追溯了公司在2018-2025年定价权时代的历史，并对流行的说法——即FICO的垄断是1995年GSE（政府支持企业）决定直接赋予它的——提出了质疑。在第二部分，我深入探讨了FICO在抵押贷款中的收费机制，详细说明了新的定价方式，并解释了评分业务拥有惊人的利润率。今天，我想在付费墙后讨论我认为对该股票最重要的问题：这个垄断究竟有多可争议？订阅者将获得每日简报以及超过五年的深度研究，即对65+公司的完整分析与财务模型。每日简报只是我在深度研究之间的随想！订阅即得。

items.author: https://www.appeconomyinsights.com/feed
items.title: 🔎 Why Google is Selling $85B of Itself
items.textEn: Welcome to the Premium edition of How They Make Money . Over 300,000 subscribers turn to us for business and investment insights. In case you missed it: 🤖 NVIDIA: Gone Parabolic 🚀 How SpaceX Makes Money 🔎 Google: The Anthropic Paradox 📈 Stock Buybacks & Dividends Explained Subscribe now Alphabet sold $85 billion of stock in two days That’s more than what the largest IPO in history will raise. SpaceX is about to price the largest initial public offering ever at $75 billion , valuing the company at ~$1.8 trillion. But Alphabet, the parent company of Google, quietly topped that last week by selling new shares — upsizing a planned $80 billion raise to $85 billion after demand poured in. Berkshire Hathaway anchored it with a $10 billion investment, buying shares at a 6–8% discount. Here’s the paradox. Alphabet sits on well over $120 billion in cash and short-term investments on its balance sheet, just generated $165 billion in operating cash flow last year, and can borrow at rates few companies on earth can match. Firms in that position almost never sell equity. Instead, they use their cash flow to buy back stock. Diluting your own shareholders is the most expensive money there is, a slice of every future dollar of profit, forever. So why do it? Because the AI buildout has grown so large it requires every available tool. Alphabet expects to spend $190 billion in 2026 alone, and that figure could increase by year-end. Every company racing to build compute now stands in front of three doors: Pay with the cash you already generate. Borrow it (issue debt). Sell a piece of the company (issue equity). Each door sends a different signal about how confident you are and how much risk you are willing to take (more on this in a minute). Today, we are looking at the three ways Big Tech is currently funding the trillion-dollar AI buildout. We’ll review which ones are good or bad for investors and what it says about where we are in this market cycle. 💵 Self-funding 🏦 Debt issuance 📉 Selling more stock 1. 💵 Self-funding The cleanest way to fund a data center is to pay for it with money the business already makes. No interest, no dilution, no banker fees. For most of this cycle, that’s exactly what Big Tech did. If you need a reminder, free cash flow (FCF) is the cash generated from the business, less capital expenditures (funds to buy, upgrade, or maintain physical assets). As long as FCF is positive, you are effectively self-funding your business ventures. Free Cash Flow = [Cash from Operations – Capital Expenditures] Amazon is the textbook case for how far that model stretches. The company is on track for roughly $200 billion in capital expenditures in 2026 , up from a trailing-twelve-month figure of $147 billion (+67% Y/Y). Meanwhile, trailing-12-month operating cash flow grew to $149 billion (+30% Y/Y) at the end of March. It’s a staggering number that, a few years ago, would have funded the whole thing with room to spare. It no longer does. Amazon is now spending essentially every dollar it generates. It was already the case in 2021 and 2022, when the company invested in fulfillment to meet pandemic-driven demand. As a result, FCF turned slightly negative in 2021 and 2022. FCF recovered to a record $38 billion in 2024 before the AI buildout cut it to $11 billion in 2025 At the end of Q1 2026, trailing FCF worsened to just ~$1 billion. "The faster AWS grows, the more short-term CapEx we’ll spend." That was CEO Andy Jassy on the Q1 call, and it captures the squeeze. AWS reaccelerated to +28% Y/Y, its fastest in nearly four years, but Amazon has to lay out cash for land, power, chips, and servers 6 to 24 months before it can bill a customer. Growth and free cash flow are pulling in opposite directions. Self-funding is a position of strength : you don’t need anyone’s permission to build. The catch is that it caps your spend at what the business produces today. The AI bill is now testing that ceiling everywhere. Microsoft, Meta, and Alphabet still fund most of their CapEx from existing operations. The open question is what happens when one great business can no longer feed an even hungrier one. Takeaway: Free cash flow is the cheapest capital and the proudest signal — it says the core business can pay its own way. But when CapEx consumes more than 100% of operating cash flow, the wallet is empty, and the next dollar has to come from somewhere else. 2. 🏦 Debt issuance Read more
items.summaryZh: Alphabet在两天内增发85亿美元股票，创下历史最高规模的融资，背后原因是其庞大的AI投入需要巨额资金。对投资者而言，这显示出即使现金充裕的公司在AI赛道也可能选择稀释股本，以快速获取所需资本。
items.translationZh:
标题：🔎 为什么谷歌要出售价值850亿美元的公司股份

正文：欢迎来到《他们是如何赚钱的》高级版。超过30万订阅者依赖我们获取商业和投资洞见。若您错过了以下内容：👀 NVIDIA：暴涨 🚀 SpaceX 如何赚钱 🔍 谷歌：Anthropic 悖论 📈 股票回购与分红解析 立即订阅。

Alphabet 在两天内出售了价值850亿美元的股票，这一金额超过了历史上最大IPO的募集规模。SpaceX 即将以750亿美元的估值进行有史以来最大规模的首次公开发行，估值约1.8万亿美元。但Alphabet——谷歌的母公司——上周悄然超越，因需求热烈将计划中的800亿美元融资扩大至850亿美元。伯克希尔·哈撒韦以100亿美元的投资坐镇锚定，按6%‑8%的折扣买入股份。

这里有个悖论：Alphabet 的资产负债表上拥有超过1200亿美元的现金及短期投资，去年产生了1650亿美元的运营现金流，且可以以极低利率借款——几乎是地球上任何公司都无法匹敌的融资条件。处于这种位置的公司几乎从不增发股票，通常会使用现金流回购股份。稀释现有股东是最昂贵的资金，因为它占据了未来每一美元利润的份额，且是永久性的。那么为何要这么做？因为AI建设规模庞大，需要动用一切可用工具。Alphabet 预计仅在2026年就要支出1900亿美元，年终可能更高。

如今，所有争夺算力的公司面前都有三扇门可选：用已有现金支付（自筹资金），发行债务，或增发股份。每扇门都传递出不同的信心和风险承受度（稍后会详细说明）。本期我们将审视大型科技公司为万亿美元AI建设融资的三种方式，并评估对投资者的利弊以及它们揭示的市场周期位置。

💰 自筹资金 🏦 债务发行 📈 增发股份

1️⃣ 💰 自筹资金
自筹资金是指用业务已产生的现金来支付数据中心等投资，无需付息、稀释或银行费用。在本轮周期中，大部分大型科技公司一直采用这种方式。如果需要提醒，自由现金流（Free Cash Flow，FCF）是指业务产生的现金减去资本支出（CapEx，购买、升级或维护实体资产的资金）。只要FCF为正，就相当于自行为业务投入提供资金。

自由现金流 = [运营现金流 – 资本支出]

亚马逊是该模型能够走多远的教科书案例。公司预计2026年资本支出约为2000亿美元，高于过去12个月的1470亿美元（同比增长67%）。与此同时，过去12个月的运营现金流在3月底增长至1490亿美元（同比增长30%）。这是一组令人震惊的数字，几年前本可以轻松融资全额且还有盈余。但如今已经不再如此。亚马逊现在几乎把每一美元收入都用于支出。早在2021、2022年，公司已因应疫情需求在履约网络上投入巨资，导致2021、2022年自由现金流略为负值。自由现金流在2024年回升至创纪录的380亿美元，随后因AI投入在2025年跌至110亿美元。2026年第一季度末，滚动自由现金流进一步恶化至约10亿美元。

“AWS增长越快，短期资本支出就越多。” 这句话来自CEO Andy Jassy在第一季度电话会议上的发言，概括了资金紧张的局面。AWS的增长率在过去一年加速至28%（同比），为近四年来最快，但亚马逊必须提前6至24个月为土地、电力、芯片和服务器预留现金，才能在客户计费。增长与自由现金流呈相反方向。

自筹资金是一种实力的象征：你不需要任何人的许可即可建设。但它的局限在于将支出上限限制在当前业务产出。当AI费用超过100%运营现金流时，钱包就会空空，需要从其他渠道寻找下一美元。

微软、Meta和Alphabet仍然主要靠现有业务为大多数资本支出提供资金。关键问题是，当一家优秀的企业再也无法为更为饥饿的业务提供资金时会怎样。

要点：自由现金流是最廉价的资本，也是最自豪的信号——它表明核心业务可以自给自足。但当资本支出超过运营现金流的100%时，钱包将见底，下一笔资金必须来自别处。

2️⃣ 🏦 债务发行
（以下内容略）
